---
title: "Primers for AD698 - Applied Generative AI for Analytics"
subtitle: "Primer 3 - Tokens and Tokenization"
author: "Nakul R. Padalkar"
institute: "Boston University"
number-sections: false
date: "2026-01-01"
date-modified: today
date-format: long
engine: jupyter
bibliography: ../../references.bib
categories: ['Tokens', 'Tokenization', 'Foundations']
description: "Introduces tokens and tokenization as a foundation for applied generative AI concepts, technical vocabulary, and later course workflows."
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    fontsize: 10pt
    css: primer-style.css
  pdf:
    toc: false
    fontsize: 10pt
    margin-left: 1.0in
    margin-right: 1.0in
    margin-top: 1.0in
    margin-bottom: 1.0in
    documentclass: scrartcl
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Tokens and Tokenization

Large language models do not directly read words, sentences or documents in the
way humans do. They read sequences of **tokens**. A token may be a whole word,
part of a word, a punctuation mark, a space-prefixed word fragment, a number
fragment, a code symbol or a byte-level unit. Tokenization is the procedure that
converts raw text into these model-readable units.

Tokenization is easy to dismiss as preprocessing, but it is one of the central
interfaces between human language and model computation. It affects how a model
handles rare words, names, numbers, programming languages, multilingual text,
context length, latency and API cost.
The type/token/term distinctions and the hard cases around apostrophes,
hyphens, names, URLs and multilingual text are standard concerns in information
retrieval and NLP [@ManningRaghavanSchutze2008IR; @JurafskyMartin2026SLP].

::: {.key-box}
**Key idea.** A language model predicts tokens, not ideas, words or sentences.
Tokenization determines the units over which probability, attention, memory and
cost are computed.
:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-tokenization-panels
#| fig-cap: "Three views of tokenization. (a) Text is segmented into model-readable units that may differ from words. (b) Different kinds of strings expand into different token counts. (c) A fixed context window creates a budget: instructions, retrieved evidence, examples and generated output all compete for token space."
#| fig-width: 8.2
#| fig-height: 2.65
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.rcParams.update({
    "font.size": 8,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "figure.dpi": 140
})

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(8.2, 2.65))

# Panel a: token boxes
ax = axes[0]
ax.axis("off")
ax.text(0.02, 0.93, "a", fontweight="bold", transform=ax.transAxes)
ax.set_title("Words are not always tokens", pad=4)
tokens = ["Gener", "ative", " AI", " helps", " analysts", "."]
widths = [0.16, 0.15, 0.11, 0.16, 0.22, 0.06]
x = 0.05
for tok, w in zip(tokens, widths):
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((x, 0.52), w, 0.20, fill=False, ec="#111827", lw=1.0))
    ax.text(x + w / 2, 0.62, tok, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8)
    x += w + 0.012
ax.text(0.05, 0.33, "Raw text: Generative AI helps analysts.", fontsize=8)
ax.text(0.05, 0.20, "Model input: integer token ids", fontsize=8, color="#374151")

# Panel b: token expansion by string type
ax = axes[1]
labels = ["plain\nsentence", "rare\nname", "number\nstring", "code\nsnippet", "mixed\nlanguage"]
counts = [7, 11, 15, 21, 18]
colors = ["#2563eb", "#3b82f6", "#64748b", "#f97316", "#047857"]
ax.bar(np.arange(len(labels)), counts, color=colors)
ax.set_xticks(np.arange(len(labels)))
ax.set_xticklabels(labels)
ax.set_ylabel("approx. token count")
ax.set_ylim(0, 24)
ax.set_title("Strings expand differently", pad=4)
ax.text(-0.45, 22.4, "b", fontweight="bold")

# Panel c: context budget
ax = axes[2]
parts = ["system", "prompt", "examples", "retrieval", "output"]
sizes = np.array([10, 14, 22, 38, 16])
left = 0
palette = ["#111827", "#2563eb", "#64748b", "#047857", "#f97316"]
for part, size, color in zip(parts, sizes, palette):
    ax.barh([0], [size], left=left, color=color, height=0.34)
    ax.text(left + size / 2, 0, part, ha="center", va="center", color="white", fontsize=7)
    left += size
ax.set_xlim(0, 100)
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlabel("token budget (%)")
ax.set_title("Context is a finite budget", pad=4)
ax.text(1, 0.32, "c", fontweight="bold")

fig.tight_layout(w_pad=1.0)
plt.show()
```

**Figure 1a** shows why tokenization is not the same as word splitting. A
single word such as `Generative` may be split into subword pieces, while a
common phrase fragment such as ` helps` may appear as one token. Many tokenizers
encode leading spaces as part of the token because spaces are informative in
text and code.

**Figure 1b** shows that token count depends on the character pattern, not only
on the number of words. Rare proper nouns, long numeric strings, code snippets,
URLs, tables and multilingual text often consume more tokens than a plain
English sentence of similar visual length. This matters because model context
windows and API prices are usually measured in tokens.

**Figure 1c** shows the context-budget problem. The model's usable context is
shared by system instructions, user prompts, examples, retrieved documents,
tool outputs, chat history and the model's generated answer. Adding more
retrieved evidence may improve grounding, but it also consumes space that could
have been used for examples, instructions or output.

# Why Subword Tokenization Exists

A model vocabulary cannot contain every possible word, name, typo, product code,
legal citation, URL, database identifier and programming symbol. Subword
tokenization solves this by using reusable pieces. Frequent words may receive
single tokens, while rare words are composed from smaller units.

This provides a compromise:

- **Word-level tokenization** is interpretable but fails on unseen words.
- **Character-level tokenization** handles everything but creates long
  sequences.
- **Subword tokenization** handles open vocabulary text while keeping sequences
  shorter.

The compromise is not only computational. It is linguistic. A tokenizer must
decide how to treat contractions, hyphenated expressions, product codes,
compound nouns and multilingual text. In information retrieval, these choices
determine which query strings can match which documents; in language modeling,
they determine the sequence over which the model learns probabilities
[@ManningRaghavanSchutze2008IR].

Modern tokenizers often use algorithms related to byte-pair encoding,
WordPiece, unigram language-model tokenization or byte-level variants. The
technical details differ, but the central goal is the same: represent arbitrary
text using a fixed vocabulary of reusable units [@Sennrich-ACL-2016-Neural;
@Kudo-EMNLP-2018-SentencePiece; @Devlin.Chang.Lee.ea.2018].

# Token IDs and Embeddings

After tokenization, each token is mapped to an integer ID. The model does not
reason over the string directly. It looks up a learned vector for each token ID:

$$
\begin{align}
\text{text}
&\rightarrow \text{tokens} \\
&\rightarrow \text{token IDs} \\
&\rightarrow \text{embedding vectors}.
\end{align}
$$

Those vectors become the input to the transformer. Attention then operates over
positions in the token sequence. If a phrase is split into more tokens, it
occupies more positions and requires more computation.

The practical consequence is subtle but important: a model's apparent fluency
with a word or phrase partly depends on how that phrase is tokenized and how
often related patterns appeared in training.

# Tokenization and Numbers

Numbers are often harder for language models than ordinary prose. A model may
split `12,487.95` into several tokens, and the token boundaries may not align
with place value in a way that supports arithmetic. This is one reason models
can write plausible financial explanations while making arithmetic mistakes.

For business uses that include numbers, numerical reliability should come from
tools, code or databases, not from unconstrained token prediction. A good GenAI
workflow lets the model explain, route, format and interpret, while exact
computation is delegated to deterministic systems.

# Tokenization and Retrieval

Retrieval-augmented generation systems also depend on tokenization. Documents
must often be chunked before they are embedded or sent to a model. Chunk size is
usually measured in tokens rather than words or pages.

Chunking decisions affect retrieval:

- chunks that are too small may lose context;
- chunks that are too large may dilute the relevant passage;
- overlapping chunks reduce boundary errors but increase storage and retrieval
  redundancy;
- tables and code may require specialized chunking because visual structure is
  not preserved by naive splitting.

Tokenization therefore shapes both what the model sees and what the retrieval
system can efficiently provide.

# Normalization and Reversibility

Classical NLP pipelines often normalized text by lowercasing, stemming,
lemmatizing, removing punctuation or deleting stop words. Those operations can
improve matching, but they can also destroy information. Modern LLM tokenizers
usually preserve more surface detail because generation must be reversible: the
system must be able to turn token IDs back into text.

| Operation | Useful for | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| lowercasing | reducing vocabulary size | loses proper-noun or acronym signal |
| stemming | lexical matching | conflates words with different meanings |
| stop-word removal | sparse retrieval | damages syntax and generation |
| byte-level encoding | arbitrary text coverage | longer sequences for some strings |

This is why tokenization for search and tokenization for generation are related
but not identical design problems.

# Context Windows Are Not Memory

A context window is the maximum number of tokens the model can condition on at
one time. It is tempting to treat a long context window as memory, but the two
are not the same. Context is temporary input. Memory implies durable state,
selection, updating and retrieval across interactions.

Long context windows are useful, but they create design questions:

- Which documents should be included?
- In what order should evidence appear?
- Should the answer cite exact passages?
- How much output space should be reserved?
- How should the system handle conflicting evidence?

More tokens are not automatically better. Irrelevant tokens can distract the
model, increase cost, slow response time and make evaluation harder.

::: {.pitfall-box}
**Pitfall.** Do not equate page count with model context. A short page with
tables, code, citations or unusual formatting may consume more tokens than a
long page of simple prose.
:::

# Business Use Without Losing the NLP Core

Tokenization affects common document and language applications:

| Workflow | Tokenization issue | Design response |
|---|---|---|
| Contract review | Long clauses and citations consume context | retrieve targeted passages |
| Financial analysis | Numbers may split awkwardly | use tools for arithmetic |
| Support summarization | Many short tickets create overhead | batch and cluster before summarizing |
| Code generation | Indentation and symbols matter | preserve formatting |
| Multilingual feedback | Token counts vary by language | budget by tokens, not words |
| RAG over PDFs | Layout may be lost during extraction | inspect chunks before indexing |

The most reliable systems treat tokenization as an engineering constraint. They
measure token counts, reserve output space, test chunking, and avoid assuming
that visible length equals computational length.

# Demonstration: Same Surface Length, Different Token Burden

Two strings can look equally short while imposing different modeling costs:

| String type | Example pattern | Tokenization risk |
|---|---|---|
| plain prose | `renewal delayed by legal review` | compact and predictable |
| identifier | `CASE-2026-05-AX19-7781` | splits into many fragments |
| code | `df.groupby("region")["arr"].sum()` | punctuation and symbols matter |
| URL/email | `https://...` or `name@domain.com` | long byte-like sequences |

This is why production systems measure token counts directly rather than
estimating from characters, words or pages.

::: {.takeaway-box}
**Takeaway.** Tokenization is the model's first interpretation of text. It
controls sequence length, context use, cost, retrieval design and the units over
which next-token probabilities are computed.
:::

### References

For classic tokenization and IR vocabulary issues, see Manning, Raghavan and
Schuetze [@ManningRaghavanSchutze2008IR]. For modern NLP treatment of words,
tokens and subwords, see Jurafsky and Martin [@JurafskyMartin2026SLP],
Sennrich et al. on byte-pair encoding [@Sennrich-ACL-2016-Neural], Kudo on
SentencePiece [@Kudo-EMNLP-2018-SentencePiece], and Devlin et al. on WordPiece
use in BERT [@Devlin.Chang.Lee.ea.2018].
